sentences.text: Did I tell you first that I was-- shared the Hebrew lessons with my sister Danka when I came home from the Hebrew school?
I shared lessons-- she knew it-- she was-- knew all the lessons.
And she could read it and write it, too-- the Hebrew.
Not the German.
I didn't share.
The German have two alphabets, they have the Gothic alphabet and the regular.
So it was a little bit difficult for me to bring it home and share it with her.
And I was a two year older sister, and the oldest sister was 14 years older-- already married and had her children.
So I was the guardian of my youngest sister.
And [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], watch out for the little one-- was always-- and I took it seriously later on.
The rest of my life, I took care of the little one whenever it was necessary.
But during to school-- during the year that I was going for extended education in the summers, we needed some pocket money.
We were living from the farm, mainly, with enough food and everything else.
But there were some things that a young girl-- excuse me-- wants to buy.

And Dad said, it's OK, so long as it is somewhere-- you had to stay there if you want a nanny to a Jewish family's child because they wanted you to stay in the room with the child, if they had one child.
And for my father, he had to check first if it was a kosher restaurant.
And a kosher hotel.
If it was a kosher hotel, the restaurant-- he approved of everything-- that it was OK.
And I had-- for two summers-- a little four-year, and then five-year-old boy there.
They were from Lwow, I think-- and for the whole summer-- and they paid me.
And I had the food there in the hotel, and everything-- the hotel room.
And the baby was sleeping in the same room with me so the parents could go out late at night.
Go out to concerts, whatever was available there for them-- and dances.
Then I took care of the little boy.
And then for two summers I did this.
And then later on, I found out-- I was studying sewing while my sister Zosia was a dressmaker, by the way, when she [INAUDIBLE].
I started about the war talking, but I should have told you before.
When she was married she was still doing some-- a little bit, dressmaking.
But before she got married, she had-- she was a dressmaking making business in our house.
We extended one room extra for her.
And then she married.
She still was doing a little bit.
And I learned to sew from her, from my sister.
And then-- and Danka learned to sew from her a little bit, too.
So both of us knew a little bit how to sew.
So I went to Krynica, and I went into a store that was selling Tiroler from Germany.
In Tyrol they make embroidered things on felt.
And you have to put them together.
So-- so parts of a skirt with decorated straps, or jackets with some-- under lapel-- some decoration-- embroidery, hand embroidery.
So I went in a store and asked if I could have a job there, and yes, they hired me.
And in the Jewish hotel again because I was eating there, too, and sleeping there.
So for several weeks during the summer, and sometimes-- one summer I did the whole summer there.
I worked the whole summer making-- putting together those decorative outfits for little girls and for boys.
And for older people, some of the things, too, like scarves they were making, and earn some extra money.
Let's go to the war itself.
What were your first signs of danger, that something was happening?
Well, something was happening, reading in the paper what was going on since '32, already bad things.
It started in '29-- started a lot earlier with Hitler.
But when he came to power, I think it's-- it's '32, and [INAUDIBLE].
And at one point we had-- the Jewish families knew that the German-- Jewish families didn't have it-- they had hardships already.
So some of the families invited children from Germany to come for the vacation-- the summer-- and to have a few weeks in-- in Krynica.
And why they were in Krynica?
They wanted to see some surrounding things, and people could sign up that they want to take them.
And we took them because we had the farm, and they could see the animals, and they could spend a whole day with us.
So some of the children came.
And they told us a little bit-- like 10-year-old children, 11-year-old children-- don't know very much.
But it told us, and it was first-hand information.
And then it got to-- to us already [INAUDIBLE], especially if children have to suffer.
And-- and there were some hardships, and anti-Semitism, and everything.
So we knew a little bit about it.
But not in the wildest dreams would we expect what happened after they came in.
I was 19 years old on August 24.
And they were-- they marched in on September 1, 1939.
OK.
And they were already in Czechoslovakia because the annexation, Czechoslovakia.
So they were four kilometers from us already there.
So when we escaped-- that's right-- the hardships after they came was hard-- was not so difficult because it was a short distance only to the Czechoslovakian border.
But that's how my sister escaped, too.
Because it was so close with that little boy that she could escape, Zosia.
OK.
But before you get into the escapes, let's talk about what happened when the Germans first came in your town.
The first day that they came in, they got themself a list, from the city, of the Jewish families.
They picked one man-- they decided, by their decision-- to call-- to ask-- appoint as a Jewish elder.
His name was Joseph-- I don't want to use last names because-- [INAUDIBLE] use his name in the-- in the book, too.
Joseph was appointed.
They summoned Joseph to come-- to-- to come to the headquarters and give them a complete list of all the-- of all the young Jewish people, boys and girls.
He was forced to do it.
OK.
It wasn't volunteer work.
So-- so else he would be shot.
OK.

Then the Jewish girls got from a notification, after they had the names, they had to be-- they're assigned to their quarters, and there was some of them scrubbing floors, and some of them polishing boots, or everything, in one building if they weren't too many.
And we had to go in the morning, go in there until they said, you can go home now.
We were working there.
No pay and no food, or nothing at all.
Just slave labor.
We started already there so, for the rest of it that came later, like Auschwitz, I had a little bit of preparation knowing what they were doing.
And then there were all kinds of hardships on the Jews.
As I said before, they couldn't go to another city, and they couldn't buy, and they couldn't do any-- any-- all the freedoms were taken away.
And then they decided-- but they were still going to the temple-- praying.
One day they decided-- and they were still wearing the beards and peyos, which Hasidic Jews wear.
One day they decided all the men have to come-- come to the temple, assemble in the temple.
And we heard some rumors from Germany sometimes, I think it was that they burned the temple with the Jews in it, or something.
So we were terrified.
So Father left for the temple.

And we were living close to it, so I could sneak out from the house, and I could see from our street that they were carrying the books, and then at the end of it the Torah scrolls-- had to throw them on the ground, and then light a match, and they had to have them on the Sabbath-- they wanted them.
They knew it isn't allowed to do it, and they knew they were the holy books.
So their-- their punishment on the Jews-- they had to have satisfaction all over.
There was no limit to it.
Whatever they knew, it's gonna hurt you more, then they did.
The first thing, they started with the books on that one particular Sabbath-- and burning the books.

And then they told them to remove their hats, because Orthodox Jews always wear hats, [INAUDIBLE]-- They remove their hats, and they had some [INAUDIBLE] there, who they assigned to it, to shave their beards and their peyos.
Because the-- Orthodox Jews had their heads shaved, anyway.
They were-- but they were wearing locks-- I think you call them locks-- the peyos and beards.
And then they locked up the temple-- they didn't burn the temple-- locked up the temple after all the books were burning.
And then they were making remarks to the Jews and putting them down, and calling them names and everything, while they were doing it too.
If everything-- after everything was burnt, Father came home, and I've never seen my father before-- [CRYING] I've never seen him like this.
He looked like the whole world was-- it was ended for him.
[CRYING] But we were happy that he came home, that he was still alive.
OK.
So we weren't as stressed as he was, but I felt sorry, and I felt terrible for him.
And so did Mom and Danka.
And then my sister, [INAUDIBLE] before [? that, ?] she'll tell you now, again, escaped to Czechoslovakia with her little boy, to have the surgery that was necessary to remove his tonsils.
And-- excuse me.
[DRINKS] And then they forbid us-- and came harvest time, which it was in September, it was very close to it.
We had to come to work anyway.
And we had planted potatoes, and all those greens [INAUDIBLE].

And the usually thing was that the Jewish people had some, like migrant workers here, some farmers, young people, would hire themselves out to work for-- for everybody, for gentiles, and for the Jewish farmers that-- that had, for money, to pay them by day.
And they were doing the harvest work.
The-- because we-- we were just the two of us, the two girls.
And my father and mother were already older-- older people.
So we hired them.
They were helping.
They were with them there, and we were feeding them, and paying them money.
So Mom was cooking for them to whole day.
And-- and this was forbidden.
So the harvest of cutting the greens, and everything, and treating it, and making it into flour was out of the question.
We couldn't do that.
But we still planted potatoes.
And Danka and I usually helped with the potato diggings too, sort of for necessity and sort of for fun, that we knew how do it.
So we would-- after the work-- in the fall-- that we did for the Germans, the slave labor, we would go out and take a lantern with us, you know, and-- and-- petrol-- what do you call it?
The lanterns with us.
And with the lanterns, as long as could through the night.
And then a couple hours sleep, and then in the morning go again for slave work.
So we dug out all the potatoes, and Father-- Father would come with a wagon and with-- or with the sacks, and we had the potatoes in a potato cellar.
And then-- this is all we could have, so if we needed flour or something, we had to buy.
So Danka and I started sewing.
She learned a little bit sewing from Zosia and I, because Zosia wasn't home anymore.
When she was home, she was earning some money, and she was-- she was helping because we were all one family waiting together.
One day, I remember, there was a really short on-- on flour, and there was a little piece of bread like this on the table.
And Mom-- Mom gave-- gave it to me, and I gave it to Danka, Danka gave it back to Dad, and Dad gave it back to Mom.
And it stayed on the table and dried there.
None of us wanted to touch it.
So we were pretty hungry if this [INAUDIBLE].
[CRYING]
[CRYING] And then they forbid the-- the Jews to assemble for their prayers on Saturday.
So they were-- they decided to do it anyway, some of them, not all of-- I don't remember-- I don't think it was all of them.
My uncle Baryl, my father's brother, lived in town, too, and he lived out of-- a little bit out of town, not in the center of town.
So decided maybe in this house-- a farm house-- too-- they won't notice it.
So they didn't dress up in the [INAUDIBLE] like Sabbath, just in regular suit.
And they went there for prayers.
And it was forbidden to pray.
Not only forbidden in the temple to pray-- forbidden assembling.
And this is called assembling.
So one of the Sabbaths, later on, they-- somebody betrayed them-- I don't know-- or maybe they found out by themselves-- I don't want to accuse anybody.
I don't know it for sure.
But there's two-- two German soldiers, two Nazis, walking into the room, and there they are, all the men there that were assembled.
And they said, didn't you know the proclamation, didn't you read it?
And don't you know that you're not supposed to assemble at all?
They [? not ?] didn't give an answer.
And I'm going to tell you, he says, what is going to happen to you.
Every time you're going to assemble-- you have a choice-- you want to assemble, that's OK, one of you is going to die.
And today it's going to be you.
That was my Father that they picked.
After they left-- and they were taking to a place out of town-- it's by the river.
It's called [? the Fence ?] by the river, and [?

Fence ?] was known that many of the Jews from other towns were killed there, and I think some gentiles that did something against their politics, and whatever they wished, were there, and they were shot to death.
So the Jewish elder, Joseph, the minute they left, started running from my uncle's home, all the way.
He was out of breath when he came to our-- the windows were open, it was a summer day, and I was standing by the mirror, far from the window, combing my long hair-- I had braids.
And he said, Rena run.
He said.
You know, I was the oldest.
Zosia wasn't there.
Run for your father.
They just took your father to the river-- to the [?
Fence ?] by the river.
And so I was combing my hair.
I was wearing just [INAUDIBLE] bare feet, and I was supposed to wear a white band with  the Star of David, in blue embroidered on the arm.
I forgot the Star of David, I forgot the shoes, and I let the hair just hang loose, and I started running.
While I was running-- it was a gravel road and-- and there were stones there.
But I didn't feel the pain at all.
I was just one thing in mind, I was going.
Then when I came there, I said to myself, what do I do now?
Why did Joseph called me?
What to do?
What can I do?
I'm gonna to fight with-- with German soldiers.
And then I said, oh, what am I going to say when I come home, and tell mom I just watched Father-- my father getting killed?
So I jumped in front of them, I said, you want to kill my father-- I said, I was naive-- and I said, I'm a young girl, and-- and-- maybe it's going to make some difference that I'm gonna beg.
I said, if you're going to shoot my father, you're going to have to shoot him through me.
And they started laughing, and making fun of me, calling him Jewish names-- the-- look at the Jewess, and some other derogatory names.
She thinks that we're gonna be caring about her.
And then they got the guns.

And before that incident happened, I was doing sewing, as I said, and one time a German officer came over to the window, knocked into-- they weren't allowed to come into the house, as far as we know, because they never went to any Jewish home-- and knocked on the window.
I opened the window, he said, I've got some sheets, here, could you make me two pillowcases?
Very-- could you-- didn't order anything-- would you?
I said, oh, yes, sure.
Yes, sir, yes, sir.
I took the sheets, and I made the pillowcases.
When, I said-- if you could-- when are they going to be ready, I had some of the work to do that I needed for-- for a living, to make.
So I said, is in a week OK?
Oh, yes, that's OK.
He came a week later, he picked up the pillowcases, and then he came another time for his friend to make two pillowcases.
Both times he left me some money.
The second time when he came-- I don't know if I should tell you-- he asked-- he asked my-- asked me, can we come into your house?
I was scared to death.
I didn't know, what does it mean?
The two of them, when they were the two of them.
And I said, can ask my mom and dad?
I shouldn't even have said that, this was a little dangerous with any average one of the SS there-- officers.
So I run, and said, Mom, Dad, they want to come into the house.
And said, OK.
So go into the parlor and just sit on a chair, and we'll see what they want.
So they came-- when-- when they came-- they weren't allowed to come in.
And we could have been punished for it maybe, two, if they come into a Jewish home.
But there was no choice.
So they came in, and then when they came in, Mom and Dad were sitting there, and I was standing close to the door when I walked in the last one in the room, and he said, do you have a gramophone?
You know what-- what it's-- the old fashioned-- kind-- for playing record-- recorded, to play records.
And I said, no, we don't.
Do you have a radio?
No, we don't.
He said, would you mind, Rena, dancing with me-- Miss Rena, he said.
He asked me what my name was.
My parents had to-- they were sitting like this-- and I looked at their faces, white as a sheet, just shaking, sitting like this.
Would you dance with me?
I-- I said, I'm not a very good dancer, sure.
I had no choice.
So if my-- [INAUDIBLE] is going to be whistling a tune.
And he whistled a tune until he-- till he was tired of whistling.
He danced with me, I think it was English Waltz, or was the-- the-- the Blue Danube waltz.
Was it.
And he said, thank you, thank you, Miss Rena, That was wonderful.
I said, well, thank you.
I just have to say something.
And they didn't shake hands with anybody, no parents, not me.
And then they said, thank you very much, and they left.
While I was standing there in front of with my father-- this had to be told because-- I-- I heard some voices, and I was terrified.
Their guns were already-- already pointed at us, two guns.
Both officers didn't think it's enough one, but both of them, those soldiers, I mean.
And there was some kind of officer, I don't know the ranks really good.
And in that moment there, I wasn't looking for it, and I wasn't interested.
All I wanted is to do something for my father, if I could.
And then I said, oh, I'm really losing my mind.
I heard voices from a distance.
And about two minutes later, I see two bikes, two high ranking officers on bikes, coming.
I-- these I knew their ranking because they had some medals and things like this on them.
And I recognized that was Hans Joksch was the name-- the-- the Austrian.
He was from Austria, he told me, too-- I'm from Vienna, Austria.
I don't know if he wanted to impress me, or something, but he told me that.
And the other one, he didn't tell me.
And both of them were coming, Hans Joksch and his buddy, are coming by bikes.
They stopped the bike, they took one look there and saw me and my father.
First they talked-- called my father a dirty Jew.
And how much I knew about expressions like this, I didn't know much.
I turned around and said, my father isn't dirty.
See, look at the nice white shirt he's wearing.
My mom washed it, and my mom ironed it, I said.
He's not dirty.
I didn't know what it meant at that time.
And the officers get off the bike.
He looked at me, didn't say anything.
And-- and he said, what are you doing here?
[INAUDIBLE] got time to shoot these two Jews.
Oh, he said, it's a nice warm day, so why don't you hop on the bikes on the back, and go for a beer.
Well, why don't you do the honors first, and kill them first?
Oh, you can do it tomorrow, or the day after.
You're gonna have plenty chances to do it-- to kill anybody that you want to, he said.
Hop on the bikes and we take off.
And they took off.
And I-- Dad and I stood there for just a few seconds.
I didn't dare turning around.
I turned around and looked at his face, and I was heartbroken just looking at his face.
And we started walking.
Not a word was mentioned.
And then, sort of a few steps that we made farther, we suddenly collapsed.
We had to sit down in the middle of the road because the knees wouldn't carry us-- it was his and mine, both-- we just couldn't walk any farther.
So we sat a little while.
It wasn't a busy road.
This is in a-- in a little town.
We sat a little while without a word.
We stood up, and we came home.
We walked all the way as fast as we could after this.
And came home.
And Mom was standing outside and waiting, and shaking like this, and so was Danka.
I think at that time Zosia was still home, too.
And they're waiting.
So, saved a life once.
For one time, not for long, though.
And then those two officers-- later I heard rumors that Hans Joksch-- I didn't see him afterwards-- we worked for them, and sometimes I worked in the office where he was there, too.
I was coming in.
But when we worked in his office, that was in [?
Muszyna, ?] a little place close to the-- the-- that bordered on Czechoslovakia.
And we were four kilometers farther away.
OK.
And when I came to [?
Muszyna ?] to work for him, we had the bag with us because we had to work the whole day with the little piece of bread with us.
When we were leaving for home, there was usually something in the back-- more bread-- that I assumed that Hans Joksch is the one that put it in, in our bag, putting it in.
And there were some other things, some goodies, I think.
One time there was a bar of chocolate, or something.
So we worked.
He let us do all the work [INAUDIBLE] got some.
Because he didn't want for the other ones to show that he was-- some privilege given to one Jewish girl, or two Jewish girls, Danka and I.
And we were dreaming of it, the assignment is gonna be there, so we have something.
But it wasn't always, every day, there.
And then one day we came there, and another time, and another time, and he wasn't there.
So I asked the-- the Jewish boys that worked for them, too.
And one of them thought that he heard rumors that he was sent to the front, the Russian front at that time.
That he was sent to the front.
And probably, probably, for the thing that he did for saving a Jew, and a-- and a Jewish girl's life.
But we have no proof of that.
But we assume, the whole Jewish community thought about that, that this was maybe the case.
Yes, I tried.
I tried later.

While the boys were working on-- on details of cutting wood, because we only had wood stoves in Tylicz, chopping wood for them, and cutting out boards for some shelves to make or something [? like it. ?] We were doing the cleaning and the other work.
But all the Jewish boys were working, too.
And one of the days I was going home from work, and the boys were still working.
And the officer in charge asked Alex, one of the boys, Alex, asked him, who is that?
I-- the girl with the long braids.
I had really long braids, I could almost sit on it.
He said, this is Rena, Rena Kornreich.
Oh.
And then a couple of days later, there's pounding on the door of his home, and there's that officer as drunk as barely standing on his legs, and said, bring me to Rena.
Your son-- asked Alex has to bring me-- to the father, he says.
I said, OK, I'll go wake him right away.
Why don't you come in and make yourself comfortable.
He gave him a chair.
He-- he could barely walk, sat on the chair.
And he went tell his son, Alex.
The floor was just-- on the first floor where they were living there, and said, you get dress quickly and  you run to the Kornreichs, and you tell them that-- to hide Rena.
That's-- tell them a German officer is looking for her [INAUDIBLE].
He's drunk and god knows what he's going to do to her.
When he got dressed they went there.
And the officer is sitting there, but because he was drunk, the father was trying to-- he said, what is taking him so long?
He said, he's asleep, a hard sleeper, and a very slowpoke to dressing.
He is dressing, he'll be here any minute.
So he ran all the way, it wasn't very far, he made it to warn my parents about it, and came back [INAUDIBLE].
And in between him coming back, Mother-- Mother had brought me to the attic.
Because we had a farm, we had straw on the whole top floor, there.
She brought me to the attic and hid me under-- under the straw, the farthest away from the stairs.
And lucky she did, because about five minutes later, they were pounding-- Mom and Dad stayed in their night clothes.
And when they-- when he pounded on the [INAUDIBLE] with the-- with the rifle-- pounded on the door, came-- they both looked sleepy.
And Danka was hidden under the blankets there in the room.
And-- and they came out acting sleepy-- what's going on?
We want-- I want Rena.
They said, she isn't home, she went to the other village.
You are lying, you dirty Jews, I see if that is true.
And I don't know how he made it, because Mom said he was drunk, made it up the stairs.
From the hallway was a staircase to go to the attic.
Walked up to the attic, took off his rifle again-- he was holding it-- and poked in the straw, looked around first.
And there was some cabinets for the winter with flour and sugar-- other things.
Like we have for a farm there.
And-- and-- we had a little bit of it, we didn't have so much.
But he looked at it, thinking that I'm hiding in one of those-- those things there.
So and then he went, took his rifle off and started poking in the straw.
At one point he came about four inches from my head.
And I was lying face down, Mom said, so he wouldn't hear the breathing, just in case he comes [INAUDIBLE].
And then he finally gave up after this, and went down.
And it's a miracle Mom didn't faint when-- when she saw.
Mom had to go with him, OK, and-- and to show him the hiding place.
She said, there is no hiding place.
She's not here.
He would have shot me, probably, and her at the same time.
And probably wanted to rape  me.
I don't know.
That was not allowed.
They weren't allowed to have anything in contact with Jews because it was rassenschande.
They were killing the Jews to purify the race, their race, there were no Jews because the Jewish race wasn't worthy.
So-- so-- they, luckily, in-- in our hometown, didn't do any of those things, but this-- this night he did.
So the next morning, my parents decided that the best thing is so long as Zosia is in Czechoslovakia, to get me to Czechoslovakia.
My father had a brother there, and my mother.
So-- and they knew that in the meantime, there was a boy in town that sort of liked me-- the whole incident of liking was maybe we exchanged words maybe 10 times in the whole growing up.
When we were little girls, Danka and I, we were living next door to-- our fiends, best friends, were living next door.
And one day we came out, and he was standing there, very handsome looking young-- young boy.
But a gentile boy.
OK.
And he said, can I bring you home?
I say, no, no, we don't need-- the hill is very slippery because this was on the marketplace, and our house was all the way downhill.
And it's slippery, and you might hurt yourself.
I said, OK.
So Danka one hand to me, one hand-- and walked us home.
And he did it during that winter maybe 20 times.
And then the next year, he wasn't there because he went to gymnasium to Krynica there.
So I didn't see him, I didn't talk much about him.
And then about-- when I was, I think, 15 or 16, one evening, I was going for my friend-- Danka wasn't with me that evening.
And he said, can I join you?
I said, but it isn't slippery.
It isn't winter.
It wasn't in the winter.
He said-- I was sort of joking about it.
And you know I'm not allowed to talk to you.
Because one time the-- one of the elders of the Jewish community saw me in the town, and it was just getting a little bit darker.

There was still light, but he had the lantern with him to go to the temple for, I think for [INAUDIBLE], forgot the real name for it-- and came to the temple, told everybody that Rena is standing with a gentile in the middle of the market-- was talking to him.
OK.
So Father and Mother were the absolutely upset.
